topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the administrative building hummed with a grating monotony that scraped against Leo’s nerves. He gripped the worn strap of his backpack so tightly his knuckles were white. Every folder he was handed felt like an accusation, a piece of evidence pointing toward an inevitable collapse. "So, this is it?" he managed, his voice flat, devoid of any inflection. The dean’s carefully curated smile seemed to stretch wider, mocking the gravity of the situation. Leo didn't look at the presentation slides detailing the campus restructuring; he stared instead at the scuffed linoleum floor, tracing invisible patterns with his gaze. He felt a cold, hard knot tightening in his stomach, a sensation that made his breath catch shallowly. He pushed his chair back with a sudden, sharp scrape that echoed in the cavernous room, the sound a tiny, defiant outburst against the suffocating quiet.

[story 2]

"Cut the crap," Maya muttered, leaning against the cold cinderblock wall of the common area. She didn't bother to look up as the campus announcement scrolled across the digital board—the final closure notice. Her jaw was clenched so tightly she could feel the strain in her molars. She kicked a loose pebble across the worn carpet, watching it bounce aimlessly before settling near a water fountain. Internally, a furious energy churned beneath her skin, a restless, vibrating tension that made her want to pace until her legs ached. When the advisor finally approached, Maya met his gaze, her eyes sharp and unyielding. "Are you seriously telling us this is just… fine?" she asked, her tone laced with a brittle edge that promised resistance. She stood up abruptly, gathering her things with jerky, agitated movements, refusing to offer any sign of surrender.

[story 3]

The air in the lecture hall was thick with unspoken dread. Elias sat ramrod straight, his posture rigid, every muscle coiled tight beneath his worn sweater. When the announcement came, a low, involuntary sound escaped him—a sharp, almost choked exhale that held no warmth. He didn't fidget; instead, he meticulously smoothed the lapel of his jacket, a repetitive, almost obsessive gesture. His gaze swept over the empty rows of students, cataloging the sudden absence of the familiar chaos he had grown accustomed to. A deep, simmering resentment began to build, a pressure behind his ribs that made breathing feel like a conscious, difficult effort. When the professor started the procedural explanation, Elias remained silent, his expression a carefully constructed mask of detached disapproval, his silence a heavy weight in the room.
